text:
PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) WITH CONCURRENT DIAGNOSTIC CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant, biallelic somatic loss on tumor NGS). Currently approximately 11 months into olaparib (PARP inhibitor) therapy; recently held x2 due to recurrent severe anemia requiring packed red cell transfusions. Rising PSA trend (last value 31.7 ng/mL). Re-staging to assess current extent of disease and eligibility for further systemic therapy.

TECHNIQUE:
Ga-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT: Approximately 5.4 mCi (200 MBq) of Ga-68 PSMA-11 was administered intravenously. Whole-body PET imaging was acquired approximately 60 minutes post-injection from vertex to mid-thigh. Low-dose CT attenuation correction was performed. SUV values are normalized to body weight (SUVmax reported). Images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes on dedicated workstation.

Diagnostic CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: Obtained with intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350, 120 mL IV, portal venous phase). Oral contrast was not administered. CT acquisition parameters: 120 kVp, automated mA modulation, 2.5 mm slice thickness reconstructed to 1.25 mm. Multiplanar reformats generated.

Comparison: Prior Ga-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT performed approximately 4 months earlier (at time of disease progression on enzalutamide, prior to olaparib initiation). Tc-99m bone scintigraphy with SPECT performed approximately 5 months earlier (at 6 months of olaparib therapy, showing partial treatment response). CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed at that same time point.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No intracranial or calvarial PSMA-avid lesions are identified. There is expected physiologic Ga-68 PSMA uptake in the bilateral lacrimal glands, salivary glands (parotid, submandibular, sublingual), and kidneys (used as background reference structures). No cervical or supraclavicular PSMA-avid lymphadenopathy. No suspicious cervical osseous lesions. Incidental note of mild degenerative changes in the cervical spine. A C7 vertebral body with faintly increased PSMA avidity is noted (SUVmax 4.1); on the dedicated CT this corresponds to subtle sclerotic change with cortical irregularity, new when compared to the prior CT examination. This appearance is suspicious for a new osseous metastatic focus, though the relatively low SUVmax compared to other sites leaves some uncertainty; correlation with targeted radiograph or MRI of the cervical spine may be of value.

CHEST:
Lungs: There is a left lower lobe pulmonary nodule measuring approximately 0.9 cm in longest dimension, unchanged in size compared to the prior PSMA PET/CT. Importantly, this nodule now demonstrates focal, moderate Ga-68 PSMA avidity with an SUVmax of 6.2, a value clearly above the mediastinal blood pool background. On the diagnostic CT images, this nodule is solid, non-calcified, and without associated satellite nodules or pleural reaction. No additional pulmonary nodules are identified. No pleural effusion. No endobronchial lesions. The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally.

Cardiac/Pericardium: Heart size is normal. No pericardial effusion. Incidental atherosclerotic calcification noted in the coronary arteries and aortic valve leaflets, consistent with the patient's age.

Mediastinum/Hila: No PSMA-avid mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. A few sub-centimeter mediastinal lymph nodes are present but are non-avid and likely reactive or benign in nature. No mediastinal mass.

Axilla: No axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally.

Chest Wall/Ribs/Thoracic Spine: A focus of increased PSMA uptake is identified at the left posterior sixth rib, SUVmax 9.7. On CT this corresponds to a lytic-appearing lesion with cortical disruption measuring approximately 1.3 cm. This lesion was not clearly present on the prior bone scintigraphy and represents a new osseous metastatic deposit. Multiple thoracic vertebral lesions demonstrate PSMA avidity: T11 vertebral body lesion, previously documented and noted to have responded on the bone scan performed at 6 months of olaparib therapy, now shows marked increase in tracer uptake (SUVmax 16.8, previously not quantified by bone scintigraphy but described as reduced uptake at response scan). On CT, T11 has progressed with a mixed lytic-sclerotic appearance and slight posterior cortical bowing without frank epidural extension. New PSMA-avid lesions are noted at T5 (SUVmax 14.3) and T8 (SUVmax 17.1) vertebral bodies; corresponding CT shows patchy sclerotic foci at these levels, new compared to prior imaging. No CT evidence of pathologic compression fracture in the thoracic spine, though posterior element involvement at T8 cannot be excluded on this examination and clinical correlation is warranted.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: A new PSMA-avid lesion is identified in the right hepatic lobe, segment VII. This lesion measures approximately 1.4 cm in diameter on CT and demonstrates moderate-to-intense PSMA avidity with an SUVmax of 8.9. On the portal venous phase CT images, this lesion appears as a hypoattenuating focus with poorly defined margins, without internal calcification or hemorrhage. No prior CT or PSMA PET correlate is identified, confirming this is a new finding. The remainder of the hepatic parenchyma is unremarkable without additional discrete hypoattenuating nodules on CT, though the sensitivity of CT for small hepatic metastases in this context is limited. No biliary ductal dilation. Gallbladder is unremarkable.

Spleen: Normal in size and without PSMA-avid lesions. No splenic lesions on CT.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No pancreatic mass or ductal dilation. Of note, this patient carries a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant; the pancreas was specifically evaluated and no concerning lesion is identified.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology without PSMA-avid lesions. No adrenal nodule or mass on CT.

Kidneys/Ureters: Bilateral kidneys demonstrate expected, prominent physiologic Ga-68 PSMA avidity in the collecting system and parenchyma, which is normal for this tracer. No discrete renal mass is identified beyond physiologic background. No hydronephrosis. No perinephric abnormality.

Retroperitoneum/Abdominal Aorta: No retroperitoneal or para-aortic PSMA-avid lymphadenopathy. Mild atherosclerotic change of the infrarenal abdominal aorta noted on CT.

Bowel: No bowel obstruction. No intra-abdominal free fluid. Incidental note of a small hiatal hernia.

Lumbar Spine/Sacrum: The previously identified L3 and L4 vertebral body lesions demonstrate persistent PSMA uptake (L3 SUVmax 18.4, L4 SUVmax 12.7), with CT showing mixed sclerotic/lytic morphology that appears less sclerotic and more heterogeneous compared to the bone scintigraphy performed at olaparib response. These lesions are thought to be progressing. The sacrum demonstrates new confluent PSMA uptake throughout the right sacral ala and extending to involve the left inferior sacrum (SUVmax 19.2), significantly more extensive than on prior imaging where only the right sacral ala was involved. CT corresponding changes show permeative lytic and sclerotic change throughout the sacrum bilaterally. No sacral nerve root canal involvement is appreciable on CT, though MRI would be more definitive.

PELVIS:
Prostate/Prostate Bed: The prostate gland, which was not resected, demonstrates heterogeneous PSMA avidity without a clearly dominant focus distinguishable from background in the setting of diffuse glandular disease and prior hormonal therapy. Seminal vesicles are atrophic in appearance, consistent with longstanding androgen deprivation.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: There is progressive PSMA-avid lymphadenopathy in the right external iliac chain. The largest right external iliac lymph node measures approximately 1.6 cm on CT (previously 0.4 cm at the 6-month olaparib response CT) and demonstrates intense PSMA uptake with an SUVmax of 11.3. A second right external iliac lymph node measures 0.9 cm (SUVmax 7.8). A small PSMA-avid lymph node is newly identified in the right obturator fossa measuring 0.7 cm (SUVmax 5.4). Left pelvic lymph nodes are not enlarged and show no abnormal PSMA uptake.

Pelvic Bones: The right iliac crest lesion, previously present and showing sclerotic changes on prior bone scintigraphy, now appears more extensive on CT with lytic peripheral component and increased PSMA avidity (SUVmax 15.6 compared to 11.2 on prior PSMA PET/CT). Right femoral neck/intertrochanteric lesion remains present and PSMA-avid (SUVmax 10.1). A new area of PSMA avidity is identified in the right femoral shaft approximately 4 cm below the lesser trochanter (SUVmax 8.3), corresponding to a faint permeative lytic lesion on CT; this is new compared to prior imaging. The left acetabulum lesion shows persistent PSMA avidity (SUVmax 13.4) without significant interval change in size, though CT morphology has become more heterogeneous. New PSMA-avid foci are identified in the bilateral pubic rami (right superior pubic ramus SUVmax 6.9; left inferior pubic ramus SUVmax 5.7), corresponding to subtle sclerotic foci on CT, interpreted as new osseous metastatic deposits.

Soft Tissues: No abnormal PSMA-avid soft tissue masses. No subcutaneous or muscular lesions. Bladder is partially filled and unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. NEW VISCERAL METASTATIC DISEASE: A new PSMA-avid and CT-confirmed hepatic metastasis in the right hepatic lobe segment VII (1.4 cm, SUVmax 8.9) is identified, representing visceral progression. The previously described left lower lobe pulmonary nodule (0.9 cm) now demonstrates unequivocal PSMA avidity (SUVmax 6.2), consistent with a pulmonary metastatic deposit; this lesion is stable in size but has become overtly PSMA-positive, suggesting viable/active metastatic disease.

2. PROGRESSIVE NODAL DISEASE: Marked interval worsening of right pelvic lymphadenopathy with the largest right external iliac node now measuring 1.6 cm (previously 0.4 cm at response imaging) and strongly PSMA-avid. New right obturator fossa PSMA-avid lymph node identified.

3. PROGRESSIVE AND NEW OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE: Extensive, widespread osseous progression with multiple new PSMA-avid lesions identified at: right femoral shaft, bilateral pubic rami, left posterior 6th rib, C7 vertebral body (low-level uptake, suspicious), T5, T8, and T11 vertebral bodies (thoracic spine), and progressive sacral involvement now extending bilaterally. Known lesions in the lumbar spine (L3, L4), right iliac crest, right femoral neck, and left acetabulum appear to have progressed on CT morphologic grounds despite prior partial metabolic response on bone scintigraphy.

4. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: Extensive, multi-compartment progressive metastatic castration-resistant prostate carcinoma involving the skeleton (widespread), regional and pelvic lymph nodes, liver, and lung, with all sites of disease demonstrating strong Ga-68 PSMA avidity. The pattern is consistent with olaparib-refractory disease in the setting of BRCA2-deficient mCRPC. Given the strongly PSMA-avid nature of all measurable disease at all sites, tumor burden is appropriate for consideration of PSMA-directed radioligand therapy (Lutetium-177 PSMA-617) per established eligibility criteria (all target lesions PSMA-positive, no PSMA-negative visceral lesions identified).
